title: Chapter 16: Ash and Mint Tobacco
paragraphs: Rumors in a fading world do not spread like fire, but like a thick mist crawling along the ground. They penetrate the cracks of taverns, settle on the armor of tired guards, and are absorbed into the gray dust of the Great Road.

The red-haired elf Taeron kept his word. Having reached the nearest outpost, he did not weave tall tales of a heroic battle against overwhelming forces. He honestly, looking straight into the eyes of his commanders, told of how his life had been bought by a small goblin with a smoldering stick. Some called it the feverish delirium of a wounded man. Others—a treacherous illusion of demons. But in Taeron's eyes was frozen a quiet, almost painful realization: all through its long life, his people had been despising the wrong creatures.

Yashil did not know about this. Wordly fame held no weight for him. He returned to the dampness of the Rotten Hollow and continued to live in the rhythm of the slow end of the world: sitting for long periods on the edge of the ravine, listening to the rustle of dead feather grass and waiting for night meetings with Kirah.

The werewolf Dzhanavar, to Yashil's surprise, also did not dissolve into the wastelands. The huge wolf made himself a lair in the ruins of an old windmill, whose millstones had long since overgrown with poisonous blue moss, and appeared exactly when the silence became too heavy.

"A true lone wolf does not attach himself to a place, Greenie," Dzhanavar philosophized one evening, stretching out on the broken roof of the mill and lazily picking out a bone stuck in his teeth with a claw. "I simply... strategically secured a high point. For perimeter control. A wolf does not sleep, a wolf waits. Tactics and dominance, brother. Augh."
"You just don't want to fall asleep alone in the dark," Yashil replied calmly, not taking his eyes off the horizon where the scars of the decaying skies flickered dimly.
"Low blow," the werewolf winced, closing his amber eyes. "You hit harder with words than those bandits did with steel."
She appeared in the third week after the blood had dried on the white stones of the Road.

Yashil was sitting in his usual place when the wind suddenly changed. Instead of the usual smell of ozone, rot, and burnt copper, the air brought something completely foreign. A sweetish aroma of old, faded perfume, the tart smell of smoldering mint tobacco and pine needles.
The goblin slowly turned around.

On a fallen, desiccated tree trunk behind him sat a creature that seemed woven from the contradictions of a bygone era. A graceful humanoid body was encased in a worn leather corset and torn stockings, over which hung an oversized, heavy cloak of fine elven work. Her hands were hidden in long fingerless gloves, quietly jingling with cheap copper bracelets.

But the head... It was a fox's muzzle. Elongated, red, with sharp, sensitively twitching ears and clever eyes, yellow as topaz. Those eyes looked at the world with such deep, ancient, and absolutely sincere fatigue that from one glance you wanted to apologize for the very fact of your existence.

The fox held a long bone cigarette holder in her thin clawed fingers, from which twined a stream of caustic, greenish smoke. Her dark fox lips were touched with cherry paint, and heavy eyeshadow lay on her eyelids, absolutely out of place in this dying world where every day could be the last.
She took a deep drag, released an ideal smoke ring into the ashen sky, and spoke in a voice like silk soaked in poison and old wine:
"So, you are the idiot."
Yashil blinked, not moving from his spot.

"The very goblin who rushed at cutthroats to save an elf whose people have considered your race a mistake of nature for millennia," she continued, gracefully crossing her legs. "It's impressive. Infinitely stupid, but impressive. I am Rene."

She extended a gloved hand to him. The gesture was slow, as if she were giving him time to change his mind. Yashil, looking at the elegant claws, calmly shook her hand. Rene's fingers were unexpectedly strong and cold.
"Why did you come to the Rotten Hollow, Rene?" Yashil asked quietly.
The fox took another drag, narrowing her amber eyes.

"Boredom, my dear green friend. Monstrous, all-consuming boredom from which the soul rots," she flicked ash onto the dead earth. "I've traveled half of this doomed continent. Picked the treasuries of three lords, slept in silks, broke a dozen hearts in high towers, and still I have nothing to do with myself. The world dies so slowly and unpleasantly. And here—a goblin who set out to defeat this decay with kindness. This," she pointed the cigarette holder directly at him, "is the most absurd and intriguing thing I've encountered in the last half-century."
"I'm not trying to save the world," Yashil shook his head. "It's impossible. I'm just trying not to become part of it."

"Oh, a philosopher. That is even more meaningless, and therefore—even more beautiful," the fox smiled faintly, only with the corners of her lips. There was no warmth in this smile, only the bitter irony of a being who had outlived its own illusions. "Perhaps I'll stay. I'll watch from the first row how this cruel universe breaks you. Or... how you break it."

Kirah came at night. He stepped with heavy paws on the withered grass with a grace not characteristic of such a huge creature, trying not to make noise. Seeing Rene sitting by the tiny campfire, the demon froze. Two glowing red eyes in a black skull widened. Instincts kicked in instantly: claws slid out of his fingers with a rasp, and the crimson veins on his pitch-black body pulsed threateningly.
"What... is that?" Kirah rasped, warily eyeing the fox and instinctively shielding Yashil.
"This is Rene," the goblin replied calmly, tossing a dry branch into the fire. "She is... a friend. Probably."

"I am an observer," Rene corrected lazily, not looking up from her eternal cigarette. The fire reflected in her eyes as golden sparks. "The word 'friend' implies obligations. And obligations are exhausting and ruin one's complexion."

Kirah froze, towering over her like a black mountain. He had been created to tear flesh and ruin walls, but before the naked, bored cynicism of this fox, he was absolutely defenseless. He didn't know how to react to someone who was not afraid of him.
Rene measured the giant with a long, evaluating gaze from head to toe.

"A demon of the Legion who is afraid down to his knees of hurting anyone," she stated, releasing a stream of minty smoke. Then she turned her gaze to Yashil. "A goblin who wants to save everyone. And a fox who desperately pretends that she doesn't care about anything. We all lie to ourselves, Horned One. The difference is only that I have enough style to do it gracefully."

Kirah opened his fang-filled maw, trying to find a worthy answer, but the words stuck in his throat. He sank down by the fire as a huge, clumsy shadow, trying to tuck his tail so as not to take up too much space.

The silence was broken by the snapping of branches. From the impenetrable darkness into the firelight stepped Dzhanavar, dragging the heavy carcass of a killed cave boar behind him. The werewolf dropped the prey by the fire, wiped his bloody maw with the back of his paw, and took in the gathered company. A tiny green-skinned pacifist-goblin, a giant renegade-demon suffering from soul agony, and a fox in a corset smoking through a cigarette holder.
Dzhanavar bared his teeth in a wide, eerie grin and sat on his haunches, driving his axe into the ground.

"Now this I understand," the wolf drawled in a velvety bass. "A flawlessly ridiculous pack. Do you know what the wise men in my forest used to say? It doesn't matter who stands against you. It matters with whom you share a piece of raw meat at the night fire. Augh, gentlemen of the jury. I definitely like it here."
Thus, around one dim campfire struggling with the cold wind, a company gathered that no chronicler would dare describe in their works.
A Goblin, a Demon, a Werewolf, and a Fox.

Four absolute outcasts. Each of them had been crossed out, rejected, and cursed by the unspoken laws of this world. But here, listening to the crackle of burning branches and the howl of the icy wasteland wind, they paradoxically complemented each other.

Yashil looked at the sparks flying into the indifferent, rotting black sky. For the first time in all his short life, he realized a simple but heavy truth. Perhaps, to withstand the end of the world, an army of steel-clad righteous men or ancient magic is not needed. Perhaps four broken beings who had the courage to stay together when everyone else chose to run into the darkness are enough.
